title: LLM Evaluation — Benchmarks, Automated Evals & Monitoring
description: At Staff level, harness engineering is about building robust evaluation pipelines that measure model quality, detect regressions, and guide data/architecture decisions. Covers benchmark selection, automated evals, human eval at scale, and continuous monitoring.

sections.title: LLM Benchmarks Taxonomy
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Benchmarks measure specific capabilities. No single benchmark captures overall model quality. Taxonomy: Knowledge (MMLU, ARC, GPQA), Reasoning (GSM8K, MATH, BigBench-Hard), Code (HumanEval, MBPP, SWE-Bench), Safety (TruthfulQA, HHH, Red Team). Each tests different aspects — a model strong on MMLU may fail on MATH.

bar_chart Concrete numbers (top models, 2025): MMLU (0-shot): GPT-4o 88.7, Claude 3.5 88.3, Llama 3 405B 87.9. GSM8K (8-shot CoT): GPT-4o 96.4, Claude 3.5 95.0, Llama 3 405B 94.8. HumanEval (pass@1): GPT-4o 92.0, Claude 3.5 90.2, Llama 3 405B 89.0. SWE-Bench (resolved): GPT-4o 31.0, Claude 3.5 49.0, Llama 3 405B 24.5. TruthfulQA: GPT-4o 74.4, Claude 3.5 73.0, Llama 3 405B 72.1. Key insight: MMLU is near saturation (88-90%). SWE-Bench and MATH remain discriminative. Choose benchmarks that have headroom.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Map each benchmark to a capability (knowledge, reasoning, code, safety). 2) Pick 2-3 per capability, prioritizing those with headroom (>10% gap between frontier and open models). 3) Run quarterly, not weekly — benchmark scores move slowly. 4) Track per-category scores, not just averages (MMLU has 57 subcategories).

search Interview Angle: "MMLU scores are saturated. How do you differentiate models at Staff level?" Go beyond averages: diagnostic sub-scores (MMLU in low-resource languages), signal-to-noise ratio (6 random seeds, report mean ± std), and adversarial variants (MMLU-Robust with perturbed options). A 0.5-point MMLU gap at 88% is noise; a 5-point SWE-Bench gap is real.

sections.title: Building an Evaluation Harness
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LM Eval Harness (EleutherAI) is the industry standard for reproducible LLM evaluation. It wraps model inference with task-specific prompts, few-shot formatting, and scoring. The same harness that runs MMLU can run GSM8K or HellaSwag — tasks are decoupled from models via a unified interface.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Evaluating Llama 3 70B on 10 tasks (MMLU, ARC, GSM8K, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, TruthfulQA, etc.) with LM Eval Harness takes ~45 minutes on a single A100 with batch size 32. Naive sequential eval takes 3+ hours. Parallel eval across 4 GPUs reduces wall time to ~15 minutes. Caching results avoids recomputation — 80% hit rate on second run.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Install: pip install lm-eval. 2) Configure a YAML task file with dataset, prompt template, few-shot examples, and metric. 3) Run: lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained=model --tasks mmlu,gsm8k --batch_size auto. 4) Add custom tasks: create a YAML under lm_eval/tasks/ with dataset_path, doc_to_text, doc_to_target, and metric. 5) Cache: set --cache_requests true to avoid re-downloading datasets.

search Interview Angle: "Your team needs to evaluate 5 models on 20 tasks weekly. Design the pipeline." Use LM Eval Harness with parallel GPU evaluation (4× A100). Add a task scheduler that distributes (model, task) pairs across GPUs. Cache model outputs per (model, task, seed) tuple. Store results in a SQLite database. Build a dashboard (Grafana) showing regression vs last week's run. Budget: 2 hours per full eval cycle.
sections.codeBlock:
# Parallel eval harness with LM Eval Harness
import subprocess, json
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

MODELS = ["meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B", "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B"]
TASKS = ["mmlu", "gsm8k", "arc_challenge", "hellaswag", "truthfulqa"]
GPU_IDS = [0, 1, 2, 3]

def evaluate(model_name, tasks, gpu_id, cache_dir="./eval_cache"):
    cmd = (
        f"CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES={gpu_id} "
        f"lm_eval --model hf --model_args pretrained={model_name} "
        f"--tasks {','.join(tasks)} --batch_size auto "
        f"--output_path {cache_dir}/{model_name.split('/')[-1]}.json "
        f"--cache_requests true"
    )
    subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, check=True)

eval_tasks = [(model, tasks, gpu_id)
              for gpu_id, (model, tasks) in
              enumerate([(m, TASKS) for m in MODELS])]

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(GPU_IDS)) as pool:
    results = pool.map(lambda args: evaluate(*args), eval_tasks)

# Aggregate results from cache
results = {}
for m in MODELS:
    model_name = m.split('/')[-1]
    with open(f"{cache_dir}/{model_name}.json") as f:
        data = json.load(f)
    results[model_name] = {t: data["results"][t] for t in TASKS}

print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))
# ponytail: real deployment needs retry logic and GPU memory checks

sections.title: Automated Evals with LLM-as-Judge
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Strong LLMs (GPT-4, Claude) can evaluate model outputs by scoring them against rubrics. MT-Bench (multi-turn conversation) and AlpacaEval (single-turn instruction following) are the standard benchmarks. The judge LLM reads the question, the model's response, and a rubric, then produces a score. Reference-based eval: compare against a gold-standard reference answer. Reference-free eval: judge scores directly without a reference — cheaper but noisier.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: GPT-4-as-judge agrees with human raters 70-85% of the time — roughly equal to inter-annotator agreement between two humans (75-80%). MT-Bench scores (1-10 scale): GPT-4 8.99, Claude 3.5 8.77, Llama 3 70B 8.10. Position bias: judge LLMs prefer the first response (A before B) ~65% of the time when quality is equal. Mitigation: swap order and average. Reference-free eval achieves 60-70% agreement with reference-based eval.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Choose reference-based for high-stakes (safety, factual accuracy). 2) Choose reference-free for rapid iteration. 3) Always use a strong judge (GPT-4 or Claude 3.5) — smaller judges bias toward their own style. 4) Judge rubric: provide the question, comparison responses, and a clear scoring rubric with anchor examples. 5) Mitigate position bias: run each comparison twice (A,B) and (B,A), take average score. 6) Monitor judge quality: periodically compute judge-human agreement on a held-out set.

search Interview Angle: "LLM-as-judge is biased toward verbose, sycophantic responses. How do you fix it?" Four mitigations: (1) Require the judge to extract a concise answer before scoring to normalize verbosity. (2) Evaluate on correctness rather than style — rubric should penalize fluff explicitly. (3) Calibrate by adding known-bad anchor examples to the rubric. (4) For factual tasks, use an external tool-based verifier (retrieval, calculator) instead of LLM judge. The combination reduces sycophancy bias by ~40%.
sections.codeBlock:
# LLM-as-judge with position bias mitigation
import random

def judge_pair(judge_fn, question, response_a, response_b, rubric):
    """Evaluate two responses, mitigating position bias via order swapping."""
    def _score(a_first: bool) -> float:
        a, b = (response_a, response_b) if a_first else (response_b, response_a)
        prompt = f"""Question: {question}
Response A: {a}
Response B: {b}

{rubric}

Score Response A from 1-10:"""
        score = judge_fn(prompt)  # returns 1-10
        return score

    # Average score for A in both positions
    score_a_first = _score(a_first=True)
    score_b_first = 10 - _score(a_first=False)  # B's score inverted to A's score
    final = (score_a_first + score_b_first) / 2
    return final, {"a_first": score_a_first, "b_first": score_b_first}

# MT-Bench-style rubric
RUBRIC = """Rate the response on:
1. Helpfulness (1-4): Does it address the user's intent?
2. Relevance (1-3): Is the information on-topic?
3. Accuracy (1-3): Is the information factually correct?
Give an overall score 1-10. Output ONLY a number."""

# Stats: 70-85% judge-human agreement
# 65% position bias toward first response → always swap and average

sections.title: Human Evaluation at Scale
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Human evaluation is the gold standard but expensive and slow. Two formats dominate: pairwise comparison (A/B) — which response is better — and Likert scale (1-5) on specific dimensions (helpfulness, accuracy, safety). A/B is more reliable (higher inter-annotator agreement) and easier for annotators. Likert gives finer-grained data but suffers from scale usage variation (some annotators never give 5s).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Inter-annotator agreement target: Cohen's κ > 0.6 (substantial). Typical κ for A/B comparisons: 0.5-0.7. Likert: 0.3-0.5. Sample size: 300-500 examples needed to detect a 5% effect size at 80% power (α=0.05). Stratified sampling: oversample hard examples (where model disagreement is high) by 2-3×. Cost: ~$0.50-2.00 per annotation for A/B crowdsourced, $5-15 for expert annotation. For an LLM release: 10K-50K annotations over 2-4 weeks.

track_changes Best Practices: 1) Use A/B pairwise as the primary method — 2-3× cheaper per information bit vs Likert. 2) Include golden quality checks: 5-10% of annotations are known-quality examples; reject annotators with <80% agreement. 3) Stratify by difficulty: easy (model clearly better/worse) + medium + hard (model tied). Report scores per stratum. 4) Measure Cohen's κ per annotator; retrain below-threshold annotators. 5) Track annotation latency — fatigued annotators produce worse labels after 2 hours.

search Interview Angle: "Product demands a 10-point improvement in human eval score. How many annotations do you need?" From power analysis: for 5% effect size with 80% power, N=400. But human eval has measurement noise (κ ≈ 0.6), so effective sample size is N_eff = N × κ² ≈ 144. You need ~700 annotations to detect 5% reliably. For a 10% effect (easier), ~200 annotations suffice. Always report confidence intervals, not just point estimates.
sections.codeBlock:
# Human eval pipeline: power analysis + inter-annotator agreement
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats


def power_analysis(effect_size=0.05, power=0.80, alpha=0.05):
    """Minimum sample size for A/B human eval."""
    z_alpha = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)
    z_beta = stats.norm.ppf(power)
    n = ((z_alpha + z_beta) ** 2 * 0.5 * 0.5) / (effect_size ** 2)
    return int(np.ceil(n))


def cohens_kappa(annotator_a, annotator_b, n_classes=2):
    """Cohen's κ for inter-annotator agreement."""
    agree = np.mean(annotator_a == annotator_b)
    # Expected agreement by chance (marginal distributions)
    hist_a = np.histogram(annotator_a, bins=n_classes, range=(0, n_classes))[0]
    hist_b = np.histogram(annotator_b, bins=n_classes, range=(0, n_classes))[0]
    p_a = hist_a / len(annotator_a)
    p_b = hist_b / len(annotator_b)
    expected = np.sum(p_a * p_b)
    return (agree - expected) / (1 - expected), agree


n_min = power_analysis()
print(f"Min samples for 5% effect @ 80% power: {n_min}")
print(f"Effective N (with κ=0.6): {int(n_min / 0.36)}")

# Simulated annotator agreement
np.random.seed(42)
n = 500
annotator_1 = np.random.binomial(1, 0.7, n)  # True label prob
annotator_2 = np.random.binomial(1, 0.65, n)  # Slightly worse
kappa, agree = cohens_kappa(annotator_1, annotator_2)
print(f"Observed agreement: {agree:.1%}, Cohen's κ: {kappa:.2f} (target > 0.6)")

sections.title: Regression Testing in CI/CD
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Every model update (fine-tuning, RAG change, prompt change) risks regressing some capability. Per-commit eval gates prevent shipping broken models. The constraint: eval must complete in <10 minutes to fit in CI. Strategy: run a fast golden test set (200-500 diverse examples covering critical capabilities) on every commit, then run the full eval suite nightly.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Golden test set: 200-500 examples covering MMLU (50), coding (50), safety (50), instruction following (50), and model-specific (100). Full eval: 7+ hours. CI budget: <10 minutes. Use small proxy models (7B-8B) for fast iteration on prompt changes; run full eval on the target model nightly. A 1% regression on the golden set triggers a flag; >2% blocks the PR.

track_changes CI/CD Pipeline: 1) PR triggers: lint → typecheck → golden eval (<10 min) → deploy to staging. 2) Nightly: full benchmark suite (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, safety) → compare vs baseline (last week's scores, stored in SQLite). 3) Regression thresholds: green (no significant change), yellow (1-2% drop in any benchmark), red (>2% drop). 4) Auto-bisect: if red, git bisect to find the commit that caused regression. 5) Report results as a PR comment via GitHub Actions.

search Interview Angle: "You see a 3% regression on a safety benchmark but no change on MMLU. How do you investigate?" Three hypotheses: (1) Safety training data was corrupted — check the pipeline log for truncation. (2) The safety benchmark has different distribution than MMLU — examine per-category breakdown. (3) The model is more sycophantic on this checkpoint — run an adversarial test set. Use the eval harness to run only safety subcategories, then git bisect to find the training step. Fix: revert the offending data batch.
sections.codeBlock:
# CI/CD regression testing for model updates
import sqlite3, json, hashlib
from datetime import datetime

class EvalCI:
    """Minimal eval CI pipeline. Tracks golden set scores per commit."""
    def __init__(self, db_path="eval_ci.db"):
        self.db = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
        self.db.execute("""
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS eval_runs (
                commit_hash TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                timestamp TEXT,
                golden_accuracy REAL,
                safety_score REAL,
                coding_score REAL,
                metadata TEXT
            )""")

    def run_golden_set(self, model_fn, golden_set, commit_hash):
        """Evaluate on 200-500 golden examples. Target < 10 min."""
        correct = 0
        safety_correct = 0
        coding_correct = 0
        for example in golden_set:
            pred = model_fn(example["input"])
            is_correct = pred == example["expected"]
            correct += is_correct
            if example.get("category") == "safety":
                safety_correct += is_correct
            elif example.get("category") == "coding":
                coding_correct += is_correct

        n = len(golden_set)
        record = {
            "commit_hash": commit_hash,
            "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
            "golden_accuracy": correct / n,
            "safety_score": safety_correct / max(sum(1 for e in golden_set if e.get("category") == "safety"), 1),
            "coding_score": coding_correct / max(sum(1 for e in golden_set if e.get("category") == "coding"), 1),
        }
        self.db.execute("""INSERT OR REPLACE INTO eval_runs
            VALUES (:commit_hash, :timestamp, :golden_accuracy,
                    :safety_score, :coding_score, '{}')""", record)
        self.db.commit()
        return record

    def check_regression(self, commit_hash, threshold=0.02):
        """Compare vs last run. >2% drop blocks the PR."""
        current = self.db.execute(
            "SELECT * FROM eval_runs WHERE commit_hash=?", (commit_hash,)).fetchone()
        last = self.db.execute(
            "SELECT * FROM eval_runs ORDER BY rowid DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1").fetchone()
        if not last:
            return "first_run"
        _, _, curr_acc, curr_safe, *_ = current
        _, _, last_acc, last_safe, *_ = last
        drops = []
        if curr_acc < last_acc - threshold:
            drops.append(f"accuracy {- (last_acc - curr_acc):.1%}")
        if curr_safe < last_safe - threshold:
            drops.append(f"safety {- (last_safe - curr_safe):.1%}")
        return f"BLOCKED: {', '.join(drops)}" if drops else "PASS"

sections.title: Production Monitoring & Drift Detection
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Model quality degrades silently after deployment. Data distribution shifts, prompt drift, and model updates cause gradual quality erosion. Real-time metrics catch regressions before they cascade. Key signals: response length distribution, refusal rate, entity extraction accuracy, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate (4xx/5xx), and output toxicity score.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Typical production: 100K-1M queries/day. Drift detection window: 1-hour sliding window. KL divergence of output distribution > 0.1 over 1 hour → alert. p95 latency threshold: <500ms (text), <2s (code generation). Error rate > 1% → auto-rollback. Refusal rate drift: ±5% from baseline → alert. Cost monitoring: $/query should be within ±10% of expected. An undetected 10% latency regression for 1 week costs ~$7K in wasted GPU capacity for a 100K QPS service.

track_changes Monitoring Stack: 1) Log every request/response pair (sampled 1:100 at high volume). 2) Compute metrics in real-time via streaming (Flink/Kafka): p50/p95/p99 latency, token count distribution, refusal rate, error rate. 3) Hourly compute KL divergence of output distribution vs a reference period (last 7 days). 4) Alerts: P0 (error rate > 1% → auto rollback), P1 (latency p95 > 2× baseline, KL divergence > 0.2), P2 (refusal rate drift > 5%, cost drift > 10%). 5) Weekly: human eval on 500 sampled responses to catch quality issues that automated metrics miss.

search Interview Angle: "Your production monitoring shows refusal rate dropped from 5% to 1%. Is that good or bad?" It depends. Good: the model is less censorious and provides more utility. Bad: the safety guardrails may have failed silently. Investigation: sample 200 queries that should have been refused (from pre-deployment logs) and check whether the model now handles them unsafely. If safe: celebrate. If unsafe: roll back and add a regression test for those 200 examples. The monitoring pipeline should track both refusal rate and unsafe response rate (via output classifier).
sections.codeBlock:
# Production monitoring: drift detection and alerting
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import entropy
from collections import deque

class DriftDetector:
    """Tracks KL divergence of output distributions over sliding windows."""
    def __init__(self, window_size=3600):  # 1 hour at 1 sample/sec
        self.reference = deque(maxlen=window_size)
        self.current = deque(maxlen=window_size)

    def update(self, metrics: dict):
        """Update with per-request metrics."""
        self.current.append(metrics)

    def kl_divergence(self, key: str, bins=20):
        """KL divergence P_current || P_reference for a given metric."""
        if len(self.current) < 100 or len(self.reference) < 100:
            return 0.0
        ref_vals = [m.get(key, 0) for m in self.reference]
        cur_vals = [m.get(key, 0) for m in self.current]
        hist_ref, edges = np.histogram(ref_vals, bins=bins, range=(0, 1))
        hist_cur, _ = np.histogram(cur_vals, bins=edges)
        p_ref = hist_ref / hist_ref.sum() + 1e-10
        p_cur = hist_cur / hist_cur.sum() + 1e-10
        return entropy(p_cur, p_ref)

    def should_alert(self, thresholds):
        """Check all metrics against alerting thresholds."""
        alerts = []
        for metric, threshold in thresholds.items():
            kl = self.kl_divergence(metric)
            if kl > threshold:
                alerts.append(f"{metric}: KL={kl:.3f} > {threshold}")
        return alerts

# Usage in production
monitor = DriftDetector()
alert_thresholds = {
    "response_length": 0.1,
    "refusal_rate": 0.05,
    "toxicity_score": 0.08,
}
# monitor.update({"response_length": 0.3, "refusal_rate": 0.02, ...})
# alerts = monitor.should_alert(alert_thresholds)
# if alerts: pagerduty.trigger(alerts)

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• LM Eval Harness — EleutherAI's standard evaluation framework. The de facto tool for reproducible LLM benchmarks.
  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena (Zheng et al., 2023) — The original LLM-as-judge paper. MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, position bias analysis, and judge-human agreement rates.
  • SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues? (Jimenez et al., 2024) — The most discriminative coding benchmark, with real GitHub issues and test-based evaluation.
  • TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods (Lin et al., 2021) — Safety benchmark measuring factual accuracy and truthfulness.
  • Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) (Liang et al., 2023) — Stanford CRFM's multi-metric evaluation framework across 42 scenarios. The gold standard for comprehensive model evaluation.

details: Staff Engineer Summary: Harness engineering is the invisible infrastructure that powers every model decision. Four layers: (1) Benchmarks — choose those with headroom; MMLU is saturated, SWE-Bench and MATH still differentiate. (2) Automation — LM Eval Harness + LLM-as-judge give fast, reproducible results with known failure modes (position bias, sycophancy). (3) Human eval — gold standard but expensive; target κ > 0.6, stratified sampling, 300-500 examples per experiment. (4) Production monitoring — real-time drift detection with KL divergence and alert thresholds; P0 errors auto-rollback. The best harness catches regressions in 10 minutes (CI) before they become user-facing problems.
